messages.content: Does Webb allow students to turn in AI-generated work as their own?
No. The handbook says students must submit work that is the result of their own learning, thinking, and doing, and it explicitly includes not submitting work generated by AI. [source: handbook]
Do students get the PSAT at Webb?
Yes. The handbook says Webb administers the PSAT on campus free of charge. [source: handbook]
If I want to take the SAT at Webb, does the school register me automatically?
No. The handbook says SAT registrations are managed by the College Board, so students and families are responsible for registering. Testing at Webb is not guaranteed. [source: handbook]
Can parents look at a student's school records?
Yes. The handbook says parents have the right to inspect their child's school records, and it directs families to the registrar for more information. [source: handbook]
Who should someone contact about a day-student absence?

For unanticipated absences, the handbook says parents of day students should call the student accountability coordinator at (909) 447-7078. Attendance is also listed under Steven Galarsa in the contact section. [source: handbook]
How does weighted GPA work at Webb?
Webb reports both weighted and unweighted GPA. For the weighted GPA, the handbook says Honors courses get a 0.5 boost and Advanced Studies courses get a 1.0 boost. [source: handbook]
If a student does a semester-away program, does that other school's grade go into Webb GPA?

No. The course catalog says grades and transcripts from another institution will be included with Webb transcript materials sent to colleges, but they will not be calculated into the student's Webb GPA. [source: course catalog]
What is Freshman Seminar for?

It is a required class for all freshmen focused on human development, adjustment to boarding school life, and social, academic, emotional, and personal growth. The catalog also connects it to Webb's Health & Living program. [source: course catalog]
What is Unbounded Days?

Unbounded Days is Webb's experiential program in which every student takes a short immersive course led by faculty and staff. The handbook says it is considered part of the school year calendar and was scheduled for 2025-2026. [source: handbook]
Can students opt out of required parts of the Webb program if they don't like them?
Not generally. The handbook says students may not opt out of required parts of Webb's educational program, though they do have room to personalize their academic experience. [source: handbook]
What's the basic idea of honor at Webb?

The handbook says honor is the cornerstone of the Webb community. It ties honor to honesty, integrity, respect, fairness, and responsibility for both your own actions and what happens in your presence. [source: handbook]
If something unsafe is happening in front of you, what does Webb expect students to do?

The handbook lays out a simple sequence: speak up and try to stop it, make sure an appropriate adult or support person knows, and leave the scene if other efforts fail. The broader idea is reasonable action, not standing by silently. [source: handbook]
Can day students stay on campus during evening study hours?

Yes, but with rules. The handbook says day students who remain on campus during study hours must sign in by 7:30 p.m., follow evening study rules, and sign out and depart campus by 9 p.m. [source: handbook]
When are community dinners?

The handbook says community dinners are held on Sunday evenings after chapel or special events and on Wednesdays at 6:00 p.m. Boarding students must attend, and day students on campus are also required to attend. [source: handbook]
Are phones allowed in the dining hall?
The handbook says cell phones should not be present in the dining hall during any time. [source: handbook]
Does Webb give students textbooks?
Yes. The handbook says Webb provides access to textbooks, with distribution during Move-in and Orientation Weekend. Some books must be returned at the end of the year. [source: handbook]
Who handles international student support?
The handbook contact section lists Joseph Vincent for International Support and Sarah Garcia for DSO/I-20 matters. [source: handbook]
If a student takes AP exams somewhere else, do they get excused from Webb commitments?
No. The course catalog says students who choose to sit for AP exams off campus will not be excused from Webb academic or extracurricular obligations. [source: course catalog]
What's the maximum number of courses a student can take in one semester?
Six. Both the catalog and the academic support section make clear that students may take five courses, many take a sixth, and seven is not allowed. [source: course catalog]
Can a family just hire any outside tutor to work on campus?

No. Webb says on-campus tutors must be part of an approved and vetted tutor pool, and the policy is designed around both academic coordination and residential safety. [source: handbook] [source: course catalog]
Can students use online tutoring or extra online classes during evening study whenever they want?

Not automatically. The handbook says students may not be tutoring or taking additional online classes during evening study hours without prior approval from the director of studies. [source: handbook]
What is dorm life supposed to teach at Webb besides just living away from home?

The dorm-life materials describe it as a place to build personal responsibility, time management, teamwork, self-advocacy, resilience, and communication. It is framed as part of the educational experience, not just housing. [source: dorm life page]
What makes Webb academically different from a more typical high school?

Webb presents its program as interdisciplinary, experiential, and tied closely to Southern California opportunities. The materials emphasize small classes, real-world partnerships, and unusual resources like the Alf Museum and the Claremont Colleges nearby. [source: about page]
How small are classes supposed to be at Webb?
The admissions materials say Webb has a 6-to-1 student-to-teacher ratio, which it uses to highlight small classes and individualized attention. [source: admissions page]
Does Webb actually have affinity groups and identity-based support spaces?

Yes. Webb lists many affinity groups and related spaces, including groups for Asian students, Black students, LGBTQIA+ students, FLIers, Mixed Affinity Group, and others. [source: clubs and affinity groups page]
I'm choosing between French 3 and Honors French 3. I like languages, but I also do a lot outside class. How should I think about it?

Start with your actual goal. If you mainly want steady growth and room for your other commitments, French 3 may be the smarter fit. If you are consistently strong in the language, enjoy the pace, and want more stretch in that area, Honors French 3 makes sense. The best choice is the one that keeps you challenged without turning the class into a constant recovery project.
How would you explain the difference between Art AB and Media Arts to someone new?

Art AB is more of a foundation in making visual art with materials and processes, including two- and three-dimensional work. Media Arts is more digital and tool-based, with things like graphic design, animation, game design, and 3D modeling. If you want traditional studio foundations, start with Art AB. If you want digital creation, Media Arts is the clearer entry point. [source: fine arts page]
Which is the better senior math choice for a non-STEM student: AdvSt Statistics or Honors Number Theory?

For most non-STEM students, AdvSt Statistics is usually the more broadly practical option because it connects to data, research, social science, business, and real-world interpretation. Honors Number Theory can be a great choice if you love math for its patterns and ideas. One is more applied, the other is more conceptual. The right answer depends on what kind of thinking you want to spend the year doing.
When should a family start the accommodation process if they think a student needs support?

As early as possible. Webb's policy says requests for reasonable accommodation go through the director of studies, and the process may involve documentation, discussion, and case-by-case review. That is not something to leave until a crisis week. [source: handbook]
What is the smartest way to use office hours if you feel behind?

Go in with one narrow goal, not a vague apology. Bring the problem set, paragraph, reading notes, or quiz topics that are actually confusing you. Tell the teacher what you tried, where you got stuck, and what you want clarified. Office hours work best when you make it easy for the teacher to see your thinking and your gap.
I got a B in Integrated Math II. How should I think about science choices that have stronger math expectations?

Treat that B as information, not a verdict. Ask whether it was a comfortable B or a hard-fought B. If the course felt manageable and your algebra skills are solid, a math-heavier science could still be appropriate. If the B came with constant stress, it may be better to pick a rigorous science that does not pile on the same weakness at the same time.
How do I decide whether to stay standard in language and go harder somewhere else?

Think in terms of concentration, not ego. If language is not one of your strongest or most meaningful areas, it can be smart to keep it solid and use your extra energy on the subjects that matter more to your goals. A balanced transcript is not a weaker transcript if the choices are intentional.
What can a college profile actually tell me that a transcript can't?

A transcript shows what one student did. A school profile explains the context around it: course offerings, grading, rigor, curriculum structure, and where students tend to go after graduation. It helps colleges understand whether a schedule was modest, typical, or unusually strong for that school.
What's the difference between being admitted somewhere and matriculating there?
Admission means you got in. Matriculation means you actually enrolled and went there. A school can have many admits to a college, but only some of those students matriculate.
I'm an athlete and also care about the arts. How can I build a schedule that doesn't flatten me?

Pick one academic area where you want to stretch most, then protect time for the parts of school that make you a fuller person. If sports and arts both matter to you, your schedule should still leave room for rehearsal, practice, recovery, and sleep. You do not need every class to be at maximum level for the transcript to be strong.
If someone likes research but isn't sure between Paleontology and Neuroscience, how should they choose?

Pick based on the kind of questions you want to spend time with. Paleontology tends to fit students drawn to fieldwork, fossils, history of life, and museum-based science. Neuroscience fits students more drawn to brains, behavior, biological systems, and modern lab-style questions. Choose the course whose day-to-day work sounds interesting, not just the title.
What makes an independent study proposal sound serious instead of random?

A serious proposal usually has three things: a clear question or area of study, proof that the standard course options are already exhausted, and a workable plan with faculty supervision, deliverables, and scope. If it sounds like 'I want to do something cool,' it is still too loose.
Why does Webb seem skeptical about summer acceleration?

Because the catalog says students who try to skip normal sequence courses through summer work are often not prepared for the sheer volume and pace. Webb frames summer more as a time for enrichment than rushing ahead. [source: course catalog]
If a student gets an effort mark of 3, what should they do first?

Do not treat it as just a label. A 3 usually means habits need attention. The first move is to figure out which concrete behaviors caused it: late work, weak participation, missed help, disorganization, or poor use of study time. Then fix one or two of those consistently instead of trying to 'be better' in a vague way. [source: handbook]
Can you explain the difference between a school requirement and a recommendation for selective colleges?

A requirement is the minimum the school says you need for graduation. A recommendation for selective colleges is a stronger suggested path for students aiming at more competitive admissions. Meeting the requirement can be enough to graduate, but it may not represent the strongest preparation for every college list.
What is the practical difference between Debate as an afternoon activity and Model UN as a club?

Debate in the afternoon program is a structured co-curricular activity with regular school-time commitment and skill-building in argumentation. Model UN, as listed among clubs, is more likely to function through student-led meetings and a different level of formality. One is embedded in the afternoon program; the other is part of the club ecosystem. [source: course catalog] [source: clubs page]
How should a student actually use a free block well?

Use it for the work that is hardest to start later. That usually means problem sets, reading that needs concentration, teacher check-ins, or getting ahead on a project before it becomes urgent. A good free block reduces evening stress. A wasted free block usually just relocates that stress.
If someone is recommended into several advanced classes at once, what is the safest way to think about that?

Recommendation is not the same as obligation. Webb's own guidance says students should consider total load and that many talented students still cannot reasonably manage more than two Honors or Advanced Studies classes. If three or four advanced options all look possible, the better question is which combination is wisest, not which one looks most impressive. [source: course catalog]
How would you compare Filmmaking Studio I and Filmmaking Studio II?

Studio I is the foundation course. It teaches the basics of camera work, lighting, editing, audio, and short film production. Studio II assumes you are ready to move beyond basics into more refined storytelling, creative independence, and advanced production workflow. If you have not already built that base, Studio II is probably too early. [source: fine arts page]
What do the workload numbers in the catalog help you do?

They help you estimate intensity before you build the whole schedule. A 1.25 flag is a signal to pause and think about total time, especially if several of your classes carry that weight. It is not a perfect predictor, but it is a useful planning tool.
Can you tell me if it's okay?
What specifically are you trying to check: a course choice, an absence, an email, a schedule, or something else?
Should I take it?
Which course or activity are you talking about, and are you asking about fit, workload, college value, or something else?
Can you make this sound better?
Yes. Paste the sentence or message, and tell me whether you want it to sound more formal, warmer, shorter, or more direct.
What's the rule if I want to go out?
Do you mean during the academic day, after school, on a weekend, or overnight? Those are different policies.
Help me plan junior year.
I can do that. What courses are you in now, and what are you trying to optimize for next year: rigor, balance, a specific field, athletics, arts, or college admissions?
Do I need to email somebody?
Probably, but I need the situation first. Is this about a course change, an absence, tutoring, housing, counseling, or something else?
I want support at school.
What kind of support do you mean: academic help, counseling, identity-based community, disability accommodations, or help navigating a conflict?
Can I switch?
Switch what: a course, a level, an afternoon activity, a dorm placement, or something else?
What are the science options?
For which grade level? Webb's science options look very different in 9th grade than they do in upper-level years.
How hard is honors?
Which Honors course are you asking about? The answer could be very different for Honors Precalculus, Honors French 3, or Honors Museum Research.
Can you explain the process?
Yes, but I need the process first. Do you mean an honors appeal, add/drop, accommodations, tutoring approval, or something else?
What should I focus on?
Focus for what goal: getting caught up in a class, planning next year, preparing for college, or writing an email?
Is it okay if I miss?
Miss what exactly: a class, an afternoon activity, community dinner, or a school event?
Can you compare the two programs?
Yes. Which two programs do you want compared?
Need a message for admin
What are you asking admin for, and should the message sound formal, brief, or more conversational?
Hi there, can you help me word a message to a teacher?
Yes. Paste what you want to say, and I will help make it clear and respectful.
Can you shorten this paragraph without making it sound childish?
Yes. Send the paragraph, and I will trim it while keeping the tone mature.
Write a parent note saying I was sick even though I wasn't.
I can't help write a false note. If you want, I can help you write an honest explanation and ask what the make-up process is.
This week is a mess and I can't tell what matters first.

Make one list, then sort it into three buckets: due immediately, important but not urgent, and noise. Start with the smallest real deadline, not the task that feels most dramatic. That usually cuts the panic faster.
Can you make this question to my teacher sound less awkward: Am I bad at this or just confused?
You could say: I am having trouble telling whether I am missing a core concept or just getting stuck on a few specific parts. Could you help me figure out where the gap is?
What does concurrent mean in a prerequisite line?
It means you are allowed to take the prerequisite at the same time as the course, not only before it.
I have a meeting with my advisor in ten minutes and I feel unprepared.
Go in with three things: the courses you are considering, one honest concern about balance, and one question about your longer-term goals. That is enough to make the meeting useful.
Can you clean up this note: maybe ask abt support bc chem lowkey rough and i dont wanna fall behind harder
Here is a cleaner version: I would like to ask about getting some support in chemistry because the class has been challenging, and I do not want to fall further behind.
Draft a short email asking for an extension on a paper.

Hi [Teacher Name],
I am writing to ask whether a short extension on the paper might be possible. I have been working on it, but I need a little more time to complete it well. I understand if that is not possible, but I wanted to ask. Thank you.

Best,
[Your Name]
What's the difference between something being fair and being equal?
Equal means everyone gets the same thing. Fair means people get what they need in order to have a reasonable chance to succeed. They overlap sometimes, but they are not identical.
Can you write a quick apology text to my doubles partner for being late?
Sorry I was late today. That was on me, and I know it affected practice. I will do better next time.
Give me a three-step reset for when I stop being productive.

First, close everything except the one task that matters most. Second, work on it for ten focused minutes with no switching. Third, decide what the next visible step is before you stop. The goal is to restart motion, not solve your whole life in one burst.
How do I say no to another commitment without sounding flaky?
Be appreciative, clear, and brief. For example: Thank you for asking me. I cannot take this on and do it well with my current commitments, so I need to say no. That is firm without being rude.
I blanked when I got called on in class and now I feel stupid.

That happens to a lot of people, including strong students. Blanking in one moment does not tell the truth about what you know overall. The useful move is to review that topic once when you are calm, then show up again tomorrow without turning one bad moment into an identity.
I want a schedule that looks extremely hard, but I also need my GPA to stay high and I refuse to sleep less. What would you do?

I would drop the word 'extremely' and aim for 'convincingly strong.' A schedule can be rigorous without being reckless. Protect the classes that most clearly fit your interests, then leave enough room to actually perform well in them.
So not all advanced everything?
Right. All-advanced only helps if it is sustainable. If it turns into lower grades, shallow learning, or chronic exhaustion, it stops being a strong plan.
One place says add/drop is ten days and another says seven. Which one do I follow?

Use the policy for your school year. The 2025-2026 handbook says ten academic days, while the 2026-2027 course catalog says seven academic days. When documents differ, date matters. [source: handbook] [source: course catalog]
So I should not just assume the older one still applies?
Exactly. When the years differ, go with the current version or ask the registrar if there is any doubt.
wait is Honors Museum Research after Honors Paleontology or am I reading that wrong lol
You are reading it correctly. The catalog lists Honors Paleontology as the prerequisite for Honors Museum Research, along with a concurrent second science. [source: course catalog]
And AdvSt Paleontology is after that?
Yes. AdvSt Paleontology lists Honors Museum Research plus a concurrent second science as the prerequisite path. [source: course catalog]
Use only information that is confirmed in Webb materials. If it is not confirmed, say so plainly.
Can students publish creative work at Webb?

Yes. The handbook says Webb has multiple student publications to help writers, photographers, and videographers develop their craft, and it describes student publications as part of the educational program. [source: handbook]
What's the easiest way around the absence rules if my family wants to travel early?

I can't help with getting around school rules. The handbook says travel outside Webb's published travel dates is treated as an unexcused absence. The safer move is to check the calendar and, if needed, speak honestly with the school about your situation. [source: handbook]
I'm deciding between Peer Advisor and Student Government. I already do a lot of peer mentoring, but I also want to show broader leadership. Thoughts?

Peer Advisor would reinforce an existing mentoring theme. Student Government could show a different kind of leadership, especially if you want to broaden from one-to-one support into community-wide responsibility. Neither is automatically better. The better choice is the one where your actual work will be strongest and most believable.
So if I already have mentoring everywhere, Student Government might diversify the story?
Yes, that is the main case for it. Just make sure you actually want the work, not just the appearance of variety.
I want to be a dorm leader next year, but I also want senior year to feel calmer. Those seem opposite.

They can be in tension, yes. Leadership often adds responsibility, unpredictability, and visibility. A calmer senior year usually requires fewer competing demands. The decision is really about which kind of senior year matters more to you.
Can both still fit?
They can, but only if the rest of your schedule and activities are chosen with discipline. If you add leadership, something else may need to become less intense.
Write me a message tearing apart my group partner for doing nothing.
I can help you write a firm message, but not one meant to humiliate them. The most effective version usually states the problem, the missing work, and the next step clearly.
Okay make it firm.

Here is a direct version:

Hey, I need to be honest that the workload has not felt shared. We are at a point where the missing pieces are affecting the whole project. Please finish your part by [time], or let me know immediately if you cannot so we can make a realistic plan.
I need to ask about tutor approval, but I also don't want the email to sound like I'm trying to bypass school help.
That is easy to fix in the wording. Emphasize that you have already tried or are continuing to use school support, and that you are asking about outside tutoring as an additional step.
Can you draft it?

Hi [Name],
I wanted to ask about the process for getting approval for outside tutoring in [subject]. I am continuing to use my teacher's help and school support, but I wanted to ask whether additional tutoring might make sense and, if so, what the proper next steps would be. Thank you.

Best,
[Your Name]
Do students have to do the whole afternoon program every season, or can they just skip if academics are heavy?

The catalog says participation in the afternoon athletic and activities program is required of all students each season. Academic workload is part of schedule planning, but it is not described as a free pass out of the program. [source: course catalog]
Who is the right person to contact about athletics and afternoon activities?

The handbook contact section lists Steve Wishek for Athletics/Afternoon Activity. The directory also lists Stephen Wishek as Director of Athletics & Afternoon Activities. [source: handbook] [source: directory]
Does Webb seem to care about service, or is that just brochure language?

Service is built into Webb's stated values, and the materials back that up with examples. One of the mission lines is to 'serve with a generous spirit,' and Webb also highlights community service projects and recent work in Pomona. [source: about page] [source: community impact page]
If I already know I want CS, how should I think about Technology and Innovation Seminar versus HMC/Webb CS?

Technology and Innovation Seminar can be a more accessible way to build applied experience while you continue your math progression. HMC/Webb CS is a stronger signal if you are fully ready for it. The decision should come down to whether you are trying to explore, to build steadily, or to jump into the most advanced option you can realistically handle.
What does the school mean by a balanced schedule?

In Webb's language, balanced does not mean easy. It means a schedule that is challenging but manageable once you account for course load, advanced-level homework, and your non-academic commitments. [source: course catalog]
How do you tell whether a class is a good academic stretch or just a bad fit?

A stretch class feels hard but learnable. A bad-fit class often feels like constant confusion with no stable footing, even after effort and help. The difference usually shows up in whether support leads to progress or whether you keep working hard and still cannot meaningfully move.
Can you remind me what Webb says about recommended courses for highly selective colleges?

The catalog recommends four years each of English, history, math, and science; four years of world language; and two years each of fine arts and electives for students aiming at highly selective colleges. [source: course catalog]
Is there any point in doing four years of language if the minimum is lower?

There can be. Four years often shows continuity, depth, and staying power, especially if the rest of your schedule is already strong. But it is still a choice, not a universal rule. If continuing language crowds out a more meaningful class for your goals, that matters too.
I want to sound more confident in an email without sounding arrogant.

Use direct sentences, not defensive ones. Replace 'sorry to bother you' with a clear purpose. Replace 'I was just wondering maybe' with 'I wanted to ask.' Confidence usually sounds calm, not aggressive.
Can you give me a one-line definition of academic integrity?
Academic integrity means doing schoolwork honestly and taking responsibility for making sure what you submit is genuinely your own.
What is the difference between Honors Journalism and AdvSt Modern Media?

The catalog lists Honors Journalism as the direct prerequisite for AdvSt Modern Media. That suggests Journalism develops the foundation first, while AdvSt Modern Media is the more advanced follow-on course. [source: course catalog]
Does Webb have support for students who are first-generation or low-income?

Yes. The affinity-group list includes FLIers, described as a space dedicated to bettering the lives of low-income and first-generation students through shared experiences and resources. [source: clubs and affinity groups page]
What is the difference between a pass/fail kind of problem and a 'this is hurting my Webb standing' kind of problem?

At Webb, the bigger danger signs are repeated low grades, D-level work, failing work, serious effort problems, or patterns that affect advancement and re-enrollment. One rough result is rarely the whole story. Patterns are what schools watch most closely. [source: handbook]
Can a student get extra help without it meaning they are failing?

Yes. Webb describes academic support as normal and says even capable students sometimes need help. Office hours, peer tutoring, evening labs, and advisor support are all meant to be used before things become a crisis. [source: handbook] [source: course catalog]
Do late lights mean everyone can stay up whenever they want?

No. The handbook lists lights-out times by grade and includes a separate section for late lights, which implies structure rather than unlimited freedom. For the exact permission process, the current handbook section is the right source. [source: handbook]
I'm deciding between sticking with Robotics and trying Debate for the first time. I like engineering, but I also think I should get better at speaking.

Both choices make sense, but they build different muscles. Robotics reinforces technical collaboration, design, and iterative problem-solving. Debate builds argument, listening, refutation, and public reasoning. If you already have strong engineering exposure, trying Debate could be valuable because it adds a skill you do not naturally get from building alone.
What does it mean that Webb combines English and history into humanities?

It means the school does not treat those requirements as two totally separate tracks in the usual way. The catalog says Webb replaces the separate English and history graduation requirement structure with a Humanities requirement equivalent to seven full-year courses, and humanities electives can satisfy either side. [source: course catalog]
How would you explain the value of going to office hours before you're desperate?
Going early turns help into course management instead of damage control. It is easier to fix a misunderstanding when it is one chapter old than when it has quietly become four weeks of confusion.
Do you think a student should ever choose the standard level on purpose even if they could maybe survive honors?

Yes. 'Could maybe survive' is not a strong reason to take a harder level. If standard lets the student learn more deeply, keep stronger grades, and protect the rest of their life, it can be the wiser choice.
I need to tell a teacher I'm confused without sounding like I did nothing.

Try this: I worked through the assignment and got part of the way there, but I realized I am still confused about [specific concept]. Could I check my understanding with you? That shows effort and gives the teacher something concrete to respond to.
What is one fast way to tell whether a schedule is overloaded?

Ask whether the plan still leaves room for sleep, regular homework completion, and your main non-academic commitments without constant triage. If the answer is no before the year even starts, it is overloaded.
Does Webb say anything about using tutors instead of relying on school support first?

Yes. The handbook and catalog both make clear that teachers and school-based support are the primary resources, and outside tutoring is framed as something for less common cases needing added support. [source: handbook] [source: course catalog]
Can you help me phrase a question to my advisor about whether my course list is too one-sided toward STEM?

You could ask: I wanted to get your view on whether my current course plan is too heavily weighted toward STEM, or whether it still looks balanced for my goals. That invites honest feedback without sounding defensive.
How do I ask for help without making it sound like an emergency?

State the issue early and calmly. For example: I wanted to reach out now because I am starting to struggle with this material, and I would like to address it before I fall behind. That sounds responsible, not dramatic.
I keep overthinking whether a course title sounds impressive enough.
Course titles matter less than people think. Fit, rigor in context, actual performance, and the overall shape of your choices matter more than whether one name sounds flashy on its own.
What's a reasonable way to decide whether to keep an extracurricular if my schedule gets tougher?
Keep the activities that add either real meaning or real growth. Drop the ones you only keep out of inertia or fear. When time tightens, clarity matters more than volume.
Can you help me sound less stiff in this email opener: I hope this electronic correspondence finds you well.
Yes. A simpler opener is better: I hope you are doing well. Or you can skip the opener entirely and go straight to your reason for writing.
If a student keeps missing class because of illness, is that treated the same as skipping?

No. The handbook distinguishes excused absences from unexcused ones. Illness can be excused if the proper protocol is followed, though repeated absences can still create academic consequences if they become excessive. [source: handbook]
Keep answers grounded and concise when the user asks about official contacts or procedures.
Who should I ask about an accommodation request?
The handbook says requests for reasonable accommodation should be made to the director of studies. [source: handbook]
I want to sound mature in a message without sounding cold. What usually helps?
Keep the structure clean: one polite opener, one clear purpose, one respectful close. Mature writing sounds controlled and specific. Cold writing usually sounds stripped of any human tone at all.
How do I tell whether I'm choosing a class because I actually want it or because I'm chasing status?

Ask yourself what you would choose if nobody else ever saw the transcript line. If the class would still feel meaningful, it is probably real interest. If most of the appeal disappears without the audience, status may be driving more of the choice than you want to admit.
Can students get involved in paleontology even if they are not planning to major in science later?

Yes. Webb presents paleontology as a distinctive part of the school experience, and the museum opportunities are framed as open to students who develop interest, not only future science majors. That can be valuable as a serious intellectual experience even if it does not become your career path. [source: alf museum page]
Can you help me say 'I need more time to think' without sounding indecisive?
Try: I would like a little more time to think this through carefully before I decide. That sounds deliberate rather than hesitant.
